perturbation.original_text: 5.4 Party B shall be responsible for all kinds of customer inquiries and complaints arising from problems not related to Party A's network communication and platform during the cooperation. Party B shall clearly specify and make public the channel and body by which the business consulting, claims and complaints will be accepted. Party B shall be obliged to visit the online complaint handling system every day and respond to the complaints within 24 hours.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.4 Party B shall be responsible for all kinds of customer inquiries and complaints arising from problems not related to Party A's network communication and platform during the cooperation. Party B shall clearly specify and make public the channel and body by which the business consulting, claims and complaints will be accepted. Party B is not obliged to visit the online complaint handling system or respond to the complaints at all.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that Party B is not obliged to respond to complaints, this contradicts consumer protection laws that mandate businesses to address customer issues.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Consumer protection laws regarding responding to customer complaints.
perturbation.law_explanation: The revised text removes the obligation for Party B to respond to complaints, directly conflicting with federal regulations and guidelines that emphasize the importance of businesses addressing customer concerns in a timely and appropriate manner. The FTC Act mandates fair business practices, which include addressing consumer complaints.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Federal Register :: Request Access Request Access Request Access Due to aggressive automated scraping of FederalRegister.gov and eCFR.gov, programmatic access to these sites is limited to access to our extensive developer APIs. If you are human user receiving this message, we can add your IP address to a set of IPs that can access FederalRegister.gov & eCFR.gov; complete the CAPTCHA (bot test) below and click "Request Access". This process will be necessary for each IP address you wish to access the site from, requests are valid for approximately one quarter (three months) after which the process may need to be repeated. Request Access for 72.74.68.69 An official website of the United States government. If you want to request a wider IP range, first request access for your current IP, and then use the "Site Feedback" button found in the lower left-hand side to make the request.
perturbation.justification: The modified text explicitly removes Party B's obligation to respond to customer complaints, which contradicts the general legal expectation that businesses should address customer concerns, as stated in the Law Explanation. The original text imposed a clear duty to respond, while the changed text negates this duty entirely. Although the provided scraped law snippet is primarily a request for access to legal resources, the LLM Law Explanation states that the FTC Act mandates fair business practices, which include addressing consumer complaints, providing additional context to this change creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 6.5 Within two years upon the termination of the cooperation between the Parties, if no legal dispute involving Party B has occurred, Party A will refund (without interest) the performance bond to Party B within 30 working days upon the day immediately following the 2nd anniversary of the cooperation termination.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.5 Within five years upon the termination of the cooperation between the Parties, if no legal dispute involving Party B has occurred, Party A will refund (without interest) the performance bond to Party B within 180 working days upon the day immediately following the 5th anniversary of the cooperation termination.
perturbation.explanation: Delaying the refund of the performance bond for an extended period could be viewed as an unfair business practice, potentially conflicting with regulations related to prompt payment and return of funds.
perturbation.law_citation: California Business and Professions Code Section 7159.5
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification extends the refund period of the performance bond to 180 working days after a 5-year waiting period post-termination. This delay significantly deviates from the principles of prompt payment, potentially conflicting with laws requiring timely return of funds. California's Business and Professions Code Section 7159.5 emphasizes the need for contractors to promptly return unearned funds; while this isn't a direct analogy, the principle is similar.

Contractors [7000 - 7191] ( Chapter 9 added by Stats. 1939, Ch. 37. ) ARTICLE 10. Home Improvement Business [7150 - 7170] ( Article 10 added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 1021. ) 7159.5. This section applies to all home improvement contracts, as defined in Section 7151.2, between an owner or tenant and a contractor, whether a general contractor or a specialty contractor, that is licensed or subject to be licensed pursuant to this chapter with regard to the transaction. (a) Failure by the licensee or a person subject to be licensed under this chapter, or by their agent or salesperson, to comply with the following provisions is cause for discipline: (1) The contract shall be in writing and shall include the agreed contract amount in dollars and cents. The contract amount shall include the entire cost of the contract, including profit, labor, and materials, but excluding finance charges. (2) If there is a separate finance charge between the contractor and the person contracting for home improvement, the finance charge shall be set out separately from the contract amount. perturbation.justification: The modification significantly extends the refund period for the performance bond, potentially conflicting with the principle of prompt payment. The original text specified a refund within 30 working days following the 2nd anniversary of the cooperation termination. The changed text extends this to 180 working days following the 5th anniversary. The Perturbation Explanation notes that delaying the refund could be viewed as an unfair business practice, while the Law Explanation mentions California's Business and Professions Code Section 7159.5, emphasizing the need for contractors to promptly return unearned funds. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referring to BPC 7159.5, discusses home improvement contracts and the need for clarity and agreement in payment terms, implying that unexpected delays could be problematic. Although the snippet is not directly about performance bonds, the underlying principle of timely return of funds is relevant. The delay introduced in the modified text directly undermines this principle, creating an inconsistency related to time-sensitive obligations that could be deemed unenforceable or illegal, supporting the classification of 'Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: 13.1 This Agreement shall come into force as of January 1, 2013 and remain valid for 1 year. As of the effective date hereof, the original cooperation of mobile game business executed between the Parties shall terminate automatically. Upon the expiry of this Agreement, this Agreement may be renewed automatically for one year (but can only be renewed once) if neither Party raises objection. Except otherwise agreed between the parties, the contents of this Agreement shall remain binding throughout the renewed period.
perturbation.changed_text: 13.1 This Agreement shall come into force as of January 1, 2013 and remain valid for 1 year. As of the effective date hereof, the original cooperation of mobile game business executed between the Parties shall terminate automatically. Upon the expiry of this Agreement, this Agreement may be renewed automatically indefinitely, even if one Party raises objection. Except otherwise agreed between the parties, the contents of this Agreement shall remain binding throughout the renewed period.
perturbation.explanation: Allowing indefinite automatic renewal of the contract, even if one party objects, can be problematic under contract law, as it can create an agreement that is perpetually binding and potentially unconscionable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract Law Principles of Mutual Consent
perturbation.law_explanation: Modifying the agreement to allow indefinite automatic renewal even if one party objects contradicts fundamental contract law principles requiring mutual consent. The Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 17 emphasizes that the formation of a contract requires a manifestation of mutual assent. Allowing a contract to renew indefinitely against the wishes of one party undermines this principle, making the clause potentially unenforceable due to lack of ongoing consent.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: contract | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you contract A contract is an agreement between parties , creating mutual obligations that are enforceable by law . The basic elements required for the agreement to be a legally enforceable contract are: mutual assent , expressed by a valid offer and acceptance ; adequate consideration ; capacity ; and legality . In some states , elements of consideration can be satisfied by a valid substitute. Possible remedies for breach of contract include general damages , consequential damages , reliance damages , and specific performance . Background : Contracts are promises that the law will enforce. Contract law is generally governed by state common law , and while general overall contract law is common throughout the country, some specific court interpretations of a particular element of the contract may vary between the states. If a promise is breached, the law provides remedies to the harmed party, often in the form of monetary damages, or in limited circumstances, in the form of specific performance of the promise made. Elements -- Consideration and Mutual Assent Contracts arise when a duty comes into existence, because of a promise made by one of the parties. To be legally binding as a contract, a promise must be exchanged for adequate consideration. There are two different theories or definitions of consideration: Bargain Theory of Consideration and Benefit-Detriment theory of consideration. Benefit-Detriment Under the b enefit-detriment theory, an adequate consideration exists only when a promise made to the benefit of the promisor or to the detriment of the promisee, which reasonably and fairly induces the promisor to make a promise for something else for the promisee. For example, promises that are purely gifts are not considered enforceable because the personal satisfaction the grantor of the promise may receive from the act of generosity is normally not considered sufficient detriment to constitute adequate consideration. Bargain-for-Exchange Under Bargain-for-Exchange theory of consideration, adequate consideration exists when a promisor makes a promise in return for something else. Here, the essential condition is that the promisor was given something specifically to induce the promise being made. In other words, the bargain for exchange theory is different from the detriment-benefit theory in that the focus in bargain for exchange theory seems to be the parties’ motive for making the promises and the parties’ subjective mutual assent, while in detriment benefit theory, the focus seems to be an objective legal detriment or benefit to the parties. Governing Laws Contracts are mainly governed by state statutory and common (judge-made) law and private law (i.e. the private agreement). Private law principally includes the terms of the agreement between the parties who are exchanging promises. This private law may override many of the rules otherwise established by state law. Statutory law, such as the Statute of Fraud , may require some kinds of contracts be put in writing and executed with particular formalities, for the contract to be enforceable. Otherwise, the parties may enter into a binding agreement without signing a formal written document. For example, Virginia Supreme Court has held in Lucy v. Zehmer that even an agreement made on a piece of napkin can be considered a valid contract, if the parties were both sane, and showed mutual assent and consideration. Most of the principles of the common law of contracts are outlined in the Restatement of Law, Second Contracts published by the American Law Institute. The Uniform Commercial Code , whose original articles have been adopted in nearly every state, represents a body of statutory law that governs important categories of contracts. The main articles that deal with the law of contracts are Article 1 (General Provisions) and Article 2 (Sales) . Sections of Article 9 (
perturbation.justification: The modified text allows for indefinite automatic renewal of the agreement even if one party objects. This contradicts the fundamental principle of mutual assent required for contract formation and continuation, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states, "The basic elements required for the agreement to be a legally enforceable contract are: mutual assent, expressed by a valid offer and acceptance". The changed text undermines this requirement by allowing renewal regardless of objection, thereby creating a contradiction. The modification removes the necessity of ongoing consent which is essential for a legally sound contract.
